objections.description: The artifact is truncated mid-section. Section 6 (State Management) is incomplete — it defines ThemeContext's state shape and stops. Missing: ThemeContext toggle logic, persistence strategy (localStorage key, SSR-safe read), RouterContext full state shape, useFetch hook implementation spec (loading/error/data state machine, stale-while-revalidate policy or not, cache invalidation), and any other contexts. The component tree references ThemeProvider with { mode, toggle } but the spec never defines toggle's behavior (does it persist? does it emit a class on <html>? does it update CSS custom properties via data-theme attribute or class swap?). Without this, four engineers will implement four different state strategies.
objections.suggested_fix: Complete Section 6 with: (1) ThemeContext: full value shape { mode, toggle }, persistence to localStorage under key 'theme-preference', read on mount with fallback to prefers-color-scheme media query, toggle sets data-theme attribute on document.documentElement and persists, useMemo on context value to prevent re-renders. (2) RouterContext: full shape { path, params, query, push, replace, back, isDirectEntry }, state machine for navigation lifecycle. (3) useFetch hook: define the state machine explicitly — idle → loading → success | error, with fields { data, error, isLoading, refetch }, AbortController cleanup on unmount, stale data policy (keep stale data visible during refetch or show skeleton — pick one and document). (4) Document that there are exactly 2 contexts (Theme, Router) and all other state is component-local via useState/useReducer.

objections.location: Section 1: BUILD SYSTEM — esbuild JSX handling
objections.description: esbuild command is missing --jsx=automatic and --jsx-import-source=react flags. Without these, every JSX file needs `import React from 'react'` at the top (the old transform). The file layout uses .jsx extensions and modern React 18, which expects the automatic JSX runtime. If someone writes a component without the React import (which is idiomatic since React 17), esbuild will emit code referencing undefined React.createElement and the app silently fails or throws at runtime.
objections.suggested_fix: Add --jsx=automatic to the esbuild command. This makes esbuild use the jsx-runtime import automatically, matching React 18 conventions. The full command should be: esbuild src/frontend/web/src/main.jsx --bundle --format=esm --outdir=src/frontend/web/dist --minify --target=safari15,chrome89,firefox90 --metafile=meta.json --jsx=automatic

objections.location: Section 4: COMPONENT ARCHITECTURE — Persistent Tab Panels + Detail Screen coexistence
objections.description: The architecture renders all three tab panels simultaneously and conditionally mounts DetailScreen when route is #/doc/:id. But the spec doesn't define what happens to the tab panels when DetailScreen is active. Are all three tab panels set to hidden/inert AND DetailScreen is visible? Is the TabBar still visible on the Detail screen (the spec says Detail has a StickyHeader with back button, implying no tab bar, but the component tree shows TabBar outside the conditional). The Shell renders TabBar unconditionally. On Detail routes, the user sees the bottom tab bar with no tab highlighted (since Detail is not a tab), which is a confusing UX. Or does the tab bar hide? This is architecturally ambiguous.
objections.suggested_fix: Explicitly specify: (1) When route matches #/doc/:id, all three persistent tab panels receive hidden+inert. DetailScreen mounts in the same <main> slot. (2) TabBar is HIDDEN (display:none + inert) on Detail routes — Detail has its own StickyHeader with back navigation. (3) Add a `showTabBar` derived boolean in Shell: `const showTabBar = ['/', '/search', '/library'].includes(currentPath)`. (4) Document this in the component tree with a comment.

objections.location: Section 5: DATA TYPES — Signal linking pattern
objections.description: The signal linking pattern `caller's signal.addEventListener('abort', () => controller.abort())` leaks an event listener if the caller's signal outlives the fetch. When fetchWithTimeout completes normally, the listener remains on the caller's AbortSignal. Over many fetches with the same long-lived signal (e.g., a component-scoped signal reused across refetches), listeners accumulate. This is a slow memory leak.
objections.suggested_fix: Store the listener reference and remove it in the finally block: `const onAbort = () => controller.abort(); callerSignal?.addEventListener('abort', onAbort); try { ... } finally { clearTimeout(timeoutId); callerSignal?.removeEventListener('abort', onAbort); }`. Document this pattern in api.js as the canonical fetch wrapper.

objections.location: Section 3: NAVIGATION — Hash query param parsing edge case
objections.description: The hash parsing `raw.split('?', 2)` correctly limits to 2 parts, but `location.hash.slice(1)` will include any percent-encoded characters. If a search query contains '#' characters (e.g., searching for 'C# programming'), the browser will truncate location.hash at the second '#'. This is a fundamental limitation of hash routing that the spec should acknowledge and handle — either by double-encoding or by documenting it as a known limitation with a workaround.
objections.suggested_fix: Document as a known limitation: 'Hash fragments containing literal # characters are ambiguous per RFC 3986. SearchComposer must encodeURIComponent the query value (which encodes # as %23) before writing to location.hash. Decoding happens in the router via decodeURIComponent on the parsed query params. Add a test case for query strings containing #, &, and = characters.'

objections.location: Section 0: MIGRATION — Missing build step in migration sequence
objections.description: Migration step M3 says 'Build React app → dist/'. But there is no documented npm/package.json setup step. Where does esbuild come from? There's no Step M2.5 that creates package.json, installs react + react-dom + esbuild as dependencies, and adds a build script. An engineer following this migration literally cannot execute M3.
objections.suggested_fix: Insert Step M2.5: 'Create package.json in src/frontend/web/ with dependencies: react@18, react-dom@18, devDependencies: esbuild@0.20+. Add scripts: { build: "esbuild src/main.jsx --bundle ...", dev: "esbuild ... --watch --servedir=.", check-budget: "node scripts/check-bundle-size.js" }. Run npm install. Commit package.json and package-lock.json (NOT node_modules — add to .gitignore).'

objections.location: Section 4: COMPONENT ARCHITECTURE — Missing theme toggle UI
objections.description: The spec defines ThemeProvider with { mode, toggle } and the entire token system supports dark/light switching, but no component in the architecture tree provides the UI affordance to trigger toggle(). There's no theme toggle button in Shell's header, no settings screen, no reference to where the user actually switches themes. The feature exists in state but has no surface.
objections.suggested_fix: Add a ThemeToggle button component to Shell's header area, right-aligned. Spec: 44×44px tap target, sun/moon icon swap with crossfade animation (--duration-fast), aria-label='Switch to {opposite} mode'. Add to file layout as components/theme-toggle.jsx. Add to component tree under <header> in Shell.

objections.location: Section 2: FILE LAYOUT — tokens.css and animations.css placement
objections.description: tokens.css and animations.css are at the root of src/frontend/web/ alongside index.html, but the authored source (.jsx files) is in src/frontend/web/src/. This splits authored assets across two directories. When esbuild bundles from src/, it won't process CSS files outside that directory unless explicitly configured. More importantly, it creates confusion about what's source vs. output — dist/ is output, src/ is source, but the CSS lives in neither.
objections.suggested_fix: Move tokens.css and animations.css to src/frontend/web/src/styles/ and have index.html reference them at their served path. Or, keep them at root (they're global styles loaded by index.html directly, not imported by JS) but add a comment in the file layout documenting WHY: 'Global CSS files are at web/ root because they are linked directly from index.html, not bundled by esbuild. They are not JS module dependencies.'

suggestions.description: Add a `dev` mode esbuild command with --watch and --serve for local development. Without this, every change requires a manual rebuild. The dev command should skip --minify for readable stack traces and add --sourcemap=inline for DevTools debugging. Document: 'npm run dev starts esbuild watch + serves on localhost:3001. Proxy API requests to web_server.py on localhost:8000 via esbuild's --serve proxy option (or document that devs must run web_server.py separately and handle CORS).'

The spec mentions 'high-fidelity skeleton variants with fixed dimensions' but doesn't enumerate which skeleton shapes exist. Define: SkeletonKPI (matches KPIMetric dimensions), SkeletonCard (matches ResultCard/HistoryEntry), SkeletonTimeline (matches TimelineItem), SkeletonText (single line, variable width). Each skeleton must have explicit height/width CSS to prevent CLS, matching the CLS < 0.1 target in Section 1.

searchDocuments and fetchTimeline are specced to return arrays, but the component tree references '20-item pages'. The API contract doesn't define pagination parameters (offset/cursor) or response shape ({ items, hasMore, total? }). Either define the paginated response shape or document that v1 returns all results in a single response and client-side paginates (which changes the API contract significantly).

The spec is thorough on ARIA attributes but doesn't include a verification checklist. Add: 'Before shipping, verify with VoiceOver on Safari and NVDA on Chrome: (1) Skip link moves focus to main content, (2) Tab bar announces current page, (3) Route changes are announced by RouteAnnouncer, (4) Bottom sheet traps focus and announces its title, (5) ErrorState retry buttons are reachable by keyboard, (6) Status card live region announces updates without stealing focus.' This turns accessibility intent into testable acceptance criteria.
